example.text: Regular exercise is not just helpful for health—it's absolutely
for maintaining strength and mobility as we age

tips: Stronger than 'important'—implies something won't work or succeed without it. Common phrase: 'make yourself necessary' = become indispensable to others.

definition.text: To express dissatisfaction, annoyance, or discomfort about something; to

example.text: about the noisy neighbors, she decided to politely introduce herself and discuss the issue

tips: Pattern: complain about + noun/gerund ('complain about the noise'). NOT 'complain of' (archaic/formal medical usage only). Noun form: complaint.

definition.text: Felt sympathy and sorrow for someone experiencing misfortune or suffering; to feel

example.text: When she lost her job during the pandemic, her friends didn't just
her—they helped her update her resume and introduced her to potential employers

tips: Can feel negative—being pitied sometimes feels demeaning. Better to show empathy (understanding feelings) than pity (looking down with sorrow). Adjective: pitiful (deserving pity) vs. pitiable (arousing pity).

definition.text: A particular part, feature, or element of something larger; one

example.text: When evaluating a job offer, consider every
— salary, location, work culture, growth opportunities—not just the starting pay

tips: Often used in plural: 'all aspects of life'. Common collocations: every aspect, important aspect, different aspects. Not the same as 'respect' (though 'in this respect' = 'in this aspect').

definition.text: To successfully complete a course of study at a school, college, or university and receive a

example.text: with honors next spring after four years of dedicated study in biomedical engineering

tips: Verb pattern: graduate from [institution] ('graduate from Harvard'). NOT 'graduate college' without 'from' (common error). Noun: graduate student = someone pursuing advanced degrees (master's/PhD).

definition.text: A person given official authority to

example.text: held a press conference to address community concerns about rising crime rates

tips: Government/business title. Different from 'committee member' (part of a group) vs. 'commissioner' (individual with authority). Often appointed, not elected.

definition.text: A scientist who studies human history and prehistory by

example.text: carefully brushed dust from the 3,000-year-old pottery shard, hoping it would reveal clues about ancient trade routes

tips: Not the same as 'paleontologist' (studies fossils/dinosaurs) or 'anthropologist' (studies human cultures broadly). Archaeology focuses specifically on material remains of past human societies.

example.text: rapidly, so doctors decided to move her to the intensive care unit immediately

tips: Often used for health, relationships, situations, or physical objects. Opposite: improving, recovering. Formal tone—more serious than 'getting worse'.

definition.text: To stop someone or something temporarily by

example.text: someone while they're speaking in many cultures

tips: Often negative (rude interruption) but can be necessary ('I had to interrupt the meeting with urgent news'). Noun form: interruption.

definition.text: Feeling slightly angry or irritated because of something

example.text: when his colleague kept checking his phone during their important conversation

tips: Common emotional state word. Often shown through tone of voice or facial expression. Not as intense as 'furious' or 'enraged'.

definition.text: The quality or character of someone's voice that expresses their
, even when the words themselves are

example.text: Although she said 'I'm fine,' her cold
made it clear she was actually upset about something

tips: Crucial in communication—tone often matters more than words. Can be: friendly, harsh, sarcastic, warm, impatient, etc.

definition.text: Requiring immediate attention or action because of

example.text: The hospital has a special entrance for
cases that cannot wait for regular appointments

tips: Medical context: 'urgent care' = treatment needed within 24 hours (not life-threatening like 'emergency'). Opposite: non-urgent, routine.

definition.text: Feeling mental or emotional strain caused by difficult circumstances, pressure, or

example.text: During final exams week, many students feel extremely

tips: Different from 'stressed out' (more intense/casual). Can be short-term (deadline) or chronic (ongoing life situation). Healthy coping: exercise, talking, time management.

definition.text: To comfort someone and reduce their fears or worries by

example.text: nervous passengers by explaining that turbulence is normal and not dangerous

tips: Pattern: reassure + person + that-clause ('reassure her that everything is fine') OR reassure someone about something ('reassure him about the test results').

definition.text: Being managed successfully; a situation that is

example.text: Don't worry about the presentation tomorrow—I have everything

tips: Common idiom for managing difficult situations. Opposite: 'out of control' (chaotic, unmanageable). Often used to calm others' worries.

definition.text: An unexpected, serious situation involving a family member that requires

example.text: She had to leave work early due to a
— her child had fallen and needed to go to the hospital

tips: Universally understood reason for sudden absence from work/school. Usually not questioned or requires detailed explanation out of respect for privacy.

definition.text: To arrange for an event, appointment, or meeting to happen at a

example.text: Because the speaker fell ill, the conference organizers had to
the keynote presentation for the following week

tips: Prefix 're-' means 'again'. Not the same as 'cancel' (end completely) vs. 'reschedule' (move to another time). Often requires coordination with multiple people.

definition.text: To deal with or handle a task, responsibility, or problem; to

example.text: everything while you rest after your long trip

tips: Very common versatile phrasal verb. Can mean: 1) handle a task ('take care of paperwork'), 2) look after a person ('take care of my grandmother'), or 3) resolve a problem ('take care of that leak').

definition.text: To end a telephone conversation by disconnecting the call; to
by placing the receiver down or pressing the

example.text: After confirming the delivery address, the customer service representative politely said goodbye and

tips: Past tense: hung up (irregular). Can be neutral (normal call ending) or rude (abruptly cutting someone off). Not the same as 'hang on' (wait) or 'hang out' (spend casual time).

definition.text: The state of someone's health, especially when referring to illness or medical status; a

example.text: The doctor explained that her heart
required medication and lifestyle changes, but she could still live a full life

tips: Medical context: 'condition' is neutral term for health state (not necessarily serious). Can be stable, critical, improving, deteriorating. Not the same as 'disease' (specific illness) vs. 'condition' (current state).

definition.text: A healthcare professional trained to care for sick or injured people, typically under a doctor's supervision; provides

example.text: noticed subtle changes in the patient's breathing before anyone else and alerted the doctor immediately

tips: Critical healthcare role. Different levels: RN (Registered Nurse), LPN (Licensed Practical Nurse), NP (Nurse Practitioner—advanced practice). Not the same as 'nanny' (childcare provider).

definition.text: Immediately; without any delay whatsoever; happening in a

example.text: When she heard her baby cry, she woke up
even though she had been in a deep sleep moments before

tips: Emphasizes zero delay. Stronger than 'quickly' (which allows some time). Often used with emotional reactions ('His mood changed instantly when he saw the gift').

definition.text: To express regret or say you are sorry for an action that caused inconvenience, offense, or

example.text: for arriving late to the important meeting and explained that his train had been delayed

tips: American spelling: apologize. British spelling: apologise. Pattern: apologize TO someone FOR something ('apologize to her for interrupting'). NOT 'apologize about' (less common/less correct).

tips: Multiple sports facilities in one location. May include gym, pool, courts, fields. Complex = group of buildings.
Candidacy = state of being a candidate. Presidential candidate, job candidate. Qualified candidate = meets requirements.

As verb: result from = be caused by. Result in = cause. Test results, election results, exam results.

tips: Don't bother = don't make the effort. Bothersome = annoying. It's no bother = happy to help.
Complaint = expression of dissatisfaction. Complainer = person who complains often. Complain about/to someone.

Improvement = act of improving. Self-improvement = making yourself better. Opposite: deteriorate, worsen.
Interruption = act of interrupting. Rude to interrupt! 'Sorry to interrupt' = polite way to break in.
Knock over = make something fall. Knock out = make unconscious. Knock down prices = reduce prices.
Lower prices, lower volume, lower standards. Opposite: raise, increase. As adjective: lower = below.
Persuasion = act of persuading. Persuasive = convincing. Different from 'force' - persuasion uses reasons, not threats.

tips: Raise prices = increase. Raise children = bring up. Raise money = collect funds. Opposite: lower.
Re- = again. Schedule = timetable. Reschedule appointment, reschedule flight. Common when conflicts arise.

Annoy = cause annoyance. Annoying = causing irritation. Less strong than angry. Mildly upset.
Health = state of being healthy. Healthful = promoting health. Healthy diet, healthy lifestyle, healthy habits.

tips: Else + where = elsewhere. Similar: nowhere, somewhere, anywhere, everywhere. More formal than 'somewhere else'.
